summary: Officially documented TDR recovery-level control. This app write restores TdrLevel to Microsoft's documented default of 3 (recover on timeout), but Microsoft warns that TDR keys are for driver testing and debugging rather than normal end-user tuning.

setting.name: Graphics TDR Level
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can react to GPU timeouts in different ways, such as disabling detection or recovering on timeout. This setting controls that recovery mode.
setting.why_people_change_it: People usually touch it only when undoing manual GPU debugging changes or targeted driver-development experiments.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Restoring the documented default is useful when cleaning up manual graphics experiments, but this is still an advanced debugging surface rather than a normal consumer tweak.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents TdrLevel under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers and lists the supported values 0 through 3. The documented default is 3, which means recover on timeout. Microsoft also warns that these registry keys are intended for testing or debugging during driver development only.

allowed_values.label: Bugcheck on timeout
allowed_values.meaning: Windows bug-checks on a detected timeout instead of recovering.

allowed_values.label: Recover to VGA
allowed_values.meaning: Recover to VGA; Microsoft notes this path is not implemented.

allowed_values.label: Recover on timeout
allowed_values.meaning: Recover on timeout using the Microsoft-documented default TDR level.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes TdrLevel = 3 on the documented GraphicsDrivers path.

windows_defaults.label: Microsoft documented default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems restoring the documented TDR recovery baseline

states.rationale: Microsoft documents TdrLevel = 3 as the default recovery mode.

windows_defaults.notes: This record exists to document and restore the default TDR recovery level rather than to encourage normal user tuning.

recommended_profiles.label: Reset to Microsoft default
intended_for: Systems returning to the documented baseline

states.rationale: Restore the documented default recovery mode.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are removing custom TDR edits and want the Microsoft baseline back.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are treating TDR recovery levels as routine consumer tuning.
tradeoffs: The documented Microsoft default is restored.
TDR level remains an advanced driver-debugging control rather than a casual tweak.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Testing and debugging TDR during driver development
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents TdrLevel under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers, lists the supported values 0 through 3, and states that 3 is the default while warning that TDR keys are for targeted driver testing and debugging only.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes TdrLevel = 3 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact path, allowed values, and default value, and the app writes that documented default as a reset-to-baseline action with a clean rollback story.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: KeyPath : HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers; KeyValue : TdrLevel; ValueType : REG_DWORD; TdrLevelOff (0) Detection disabled ... TdrLevelRecover (3) Recover on timeout (default value).
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft's TDR registry keys page documents the exact GraphicsDrivers runtime path, the TdrLevel value name, the supported values 0 through 3, and the documented default of 3.
